behaviors.description: Content passed to summary-generation step includes data from all three sources
behaviors.justification: The failure (data from one source is missing or dropped) can be caught by unit testing with mocks of the three source APIs. We're testing aggregation/wiring logic — whether the collect_data() function actually calls all three sources and includes their results in the output passed to summary_gen(). A mock-based unit test that verifies all three mocked sources' results appear in the aggregated output is sufficient to catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the three source APIs (metrics endpoint, issue tracker, deployment log). Call the data collection function. Verify the returned data dict contains non-empty results from all three sources. Assert that summary_gen() is called with a dict that includes 'metrics', 'issues', and 'deployments' keys.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, report is produced with 'data unavailable' note for that section rather than aborting
behaviors.justification: Error handling logic across multiple components needs integration testing. Unit mocking one source to raise an exception only tests the exception handler in isolation; it doesn't verify that the *remaining* sources are collected successfully and the report generation actually proceeds. The failure to catch is: script aborts on first source failure, or error message is incorrect, or subsequent sources aren't called. Integration testing with one real mock source failing while others succeed ensures the degraded-mode behavior works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Set up mock HTTP servers for all three sources. Configure one source (e.g., metrics endpoint) to return 5xx or timeout. Call the data collection and report generation pipeline. Verify: (1) collection completes without raising, (2) the returned data includes 'data unavailable' or error marker for the failed source, (3) data from the other two sources is present and valid, (4) report is generated and contains the unavailable note.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data
behaviors.justification: The phrase 'generates a narrative summary' indicates LLM-based text generation (typical in modern systems, especially in an autonomous prompt-improvement context). The failure to catch is: the LLM produces a summary that misrepresents key metrics, inverts facts, or omits critical values. A unit test with a deterministic template or mock would always pass, missing real LLM output errors. Testing with a real LLM (or a faithful LLM proxy) is necessary to verify that the actual model produces accurate prose for the given input data.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fixed input dataset with known key values (e.g., uptime 99.9%, 5 open issues, 3 deployments, deployment duration 2h). Call the narrative generation function with a real LLM (or high-fidelity LLM mock). Parse or extract the key facts from the generated summary. Verify that all key values are present and correctly stated (not inverted, not off by order of magnitude). Consider using structured extraction (e.g., another LLM call) or human spot-check for accuracy.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly
behaviors.justification: This end-to-end test involves multiple sequential steps: (1) data collection from three sources (System/Integration), (2) LLM-based narrative generation (Agentic), (3) email delivery to SMTP (System). Since at least one step (narrative generation) is Agentic, and the failure can only be caught by running the full pipeline with real LLM calls and real or realistic email delivery infrastructure, the minimum is Workflow. A unit test of any single step would miss failures in the orchestration, LLM output quality, or email delivery logic.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a complete test harness: mock or stage the three data sources (HTTP mock servers or fixtures), use a real LLM API call (or high-fidelity proxy) for narrative generation, and use a local SMTP mock server or test email service (e.g., MailHog, SendGrid sandbox). Execute the full scheduled report pipeline. Verify: (1) all three sources are called and contribute to data, (2) narrative is generated without errors, (3) email is composed with correct To/From/Subject, (4) email body includes the narrative summary, (5) SMTP delivery succeeds and message appears in the mock inbox.

behaviors.description: When email delivery service is unreachable, generated report is saved to local file and delivery is retried on next scheduled run
behaviors.justification: This test requires real filesystem I/O and a realistic email service failure condition. The failures to catch are: (1) report is discarded instead of saved, (2) saved to wrong path or wrong format, (3) retry logic doesn't actually retry the saved report, (4) concurrent runs cause file conflicts. Unit tests with mocked filesystem would always pass. System-level testing with real local filesystem operations and a simulatable email failure (mock SMTP that rejects connection, or timeout) is necessary to verify the fallback and retry behavior works in practice.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the report generator with a temporary local directory for fallback saves. Use a mock SMTP server that simulates unreachability (connection refused or timeout). Execute the report pipeline. Verify: (1) the SMTP error is caught, (2) the generated report is written to the configured fallback directory with correct filename and format (JSON, markdown, etc.), (3) file contents are valid and complete. Run the pipeline again and verify: (4) the retry logic identifies the pending report from the previous run, (5) re-attempts email delivery, (6) cleans up the file on successful delivery (or marks it as delivered).
